title: Pwnagotchi – AI-Powered WiFi Exploration Buddy
summary: Set up and customized a Pwnagotchi, a Tamagotchi-like wearable companion that learns from WiFi environments via A2C an reinforcement learning policy. Configured plugins, tethering, and web UI access to explore & learn about common network security protocols.

title: TinkerHand – robo.innovate Hackathon
summary: For the hackathon, our team built a robotic assistant with an speech2text pipeline that selects trained ACT policies through keyword recognition. Our goal was to develop a buddy similar to 'Dummy' in Iron Man (see pic). We built this with the kind sponsoring of materials and compute of tlrc (Y-Combinator 2025).

title: G.H.O.S.T. – Tendon-Driven Robotic Hand
summary: I fully designed, built and programmed a DIY robotic hand actuated with nylon tendons. My goal was to keep my budget < 200$. For control I implemented a wireless glove that operated via self-made flex sensors.

title: Semi-Autonomous Control for Robotic Hand Grasping
summary: Our team developed a semi-autonomous control framework for a tendon-driven robotic hand. I was mainly working on the mechanical components and tuning of the grasping. We then combined vision-based object recognition to assist grasp initiation according to object classification, e.g. pen -> pinch; tv remote -> grasp.

title: Generative Classifiers as Trustworthy Alternative in Tactile Classification
summary: Our team of 2 explored invertible neural networks and generative classifiers for robust tactile material classification. We aimed to match the current state of the art, whilst improving explainability. Was a part of [CIT433027] Advanced Deep Learning for Robotics.

title: Neural Networks Control Video
summary: 3blue1brown style video about neural networks and their employment in robotic control for [MW2388] Applied Biorobotics. I tried to combine .pptx with AI voice overs to get a really professional look.

title: Robotic Arm – HackaHealth ETH Zürich
summary: Our team developed an adaptive robotic arm prototype for quadriplegic patient over the hackathon. It allowed him to pick up things and interact with his children again. 3 design iterations were funded by the ETH Student Project House.
